title: The seven Ps of the marketing mix
summary: The marketing mix is the set of controllable decisions a business makes to turn a target market into paying, loyal customers. This lesson works through the whole of syllabus 4.5: the original four Ps — product (the product life cycle, the Boston/product portfolio matrix and branding), price (cost-plus, penetration, skimming, competitive, psychological, premium and dynamic pricing), place (distribution channels) and promotion (above-the-line and below-the-line, the promotional mix, and guerrilla and digital marketing) — and the three extra service Ps: people, process and physical evidence. It shows why the seven elements must be integrated and consistent, how the mix differs for goods versus services, and — because 4.5 is an application- and evaluation-heavy topic — every worked example is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks: AO1 knowledge, AO2 application to context and AO3 analysis and evaluation ending in a supported judgement.
learningObjectives: Explain the product element of the mix, including the product life cycle, the Boston (product portfolio) matrix and branding.
Distinguish the main pricing strategies — cost-plus, penetration, skimming, competitive, psychological, premium and dynamic — and judge when each is appropriate.
Explain the role of place (distribution channels) and promotion (above- and below-the-line, the promotional mix, guerrilla and digital marketing) in the mix.
Explain the three service Ps — people, process and physical evidence — and why they were added for services.
Analyse why the seven Ps must be integrated and consistent, and how the mix differs for goods versus services, reaching a supported judgement when recommending a mix.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: The seven dials on a marketing control panel
simpleExplanation.summary: The marketing mix is a control panel with seven dials a business can turn. Product, price, place and promotion are the four original dials that fit any offering; people, process and physical evidence are three extra dials added because services are harder to sell than physical goods. Turn the dials so they all point the same way and the customer gets one clear, convincing message; leave them pointing in different directions and the whole strategy jars.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of the mix as the settings on a music mixing desk. Each slider — the product itself, its price, where it is sold, how it is promoted, the staff who deliver it, the process of buying it, and the physical surroundings — has to be balanced against the others to produce one clean sound. Push the 'premium product' slider all the way up but drop the 'price' slider to bargain-basement, sell it in a pound shop and advertise it with cheap flyers, and the track is a mess: the customer cannot tell what the brand is meant to be. A great marketing mix, like a great mix of a song, is judged on how well the parts fit together, not on any one slider alone.
steps: Define the offering — its features, quality, branding and where it sits on the product life cycle and portfolio (Product).
Set a price using a strategy that matches the product's stage, costs, competitors and customers' willingness to pay (Price).
Choose distribution channels and a promotional mix that reach the target market cost-effectively (Place and Promotion).
For services, design the people, process and physical evidence so the intangible offering feels reliable and consistent — then check all seven dials point the same way.

flashcards.back: The combination of controllable elements — product, price, place, promotion, people, process and physical evidence — a business blends to market its offering to a target market. The first four suit any product; the last three were added for services.

The four stages a product typically passes through over time — introduction, growth, maturity and decline — each with different sales, profit and marketing needs. Extension strategies can prolong the maturity stage.
A tool that plots a firm's products by market share and market growth into four categories — stars, cash cows, question marks and dogs — to guide investment across the portfolio.

Creating a distinctive identity (name, logo, values, promise) that customers recognise and trust. Strong brands add value, support premium pricing and build loyalty, turning a product into more than its physical features.
Adding a fixed profit mark-up to the unit cost of a product. Simple and covers costs, but ignores what competitors charge and what customers are willing to pay.

Setting a deliberately LOW launch price to win market share quickly and attract price-sensitive buyers. Suits mass markets and products with close substitutes; sacrifices short-term margin for volume and market presence.

Setting a HIGH launch price to target early adopters willing to pay a premium, then lowering it over time as competition arrives. Suits innovative, differentiated products and recovers high development costs quickly.
Setting price in line with, or in reference to, rivals' prices. Common in markets with many similar products where a firm has little power to charge more than the going rate.
Setting a price that appears lower or more attractive to the customer — for example $9.99 rather than $10 — using perception rather than a real cost difference to encourage purchase.
Setting a consistently HIGH price to signal superior quality, exclusivity or status. Works only when brand, product and other Ps genuinely support the high-value positioning.

Continuously adjusting price in response to real-time demand, timing or customer data — as with airline seats, ride-hailing surge fares and event tickets. Maximises revenue but can feel unfair to customers.

How the product reaches the customer — the path from producer to consumer. Channels may be direct (producer to consumer), or indirect via wholesalers and/or retailers, and increasingly e-commerce and apps.
Paid promotion through mass, largely untargeted media — TV, radio, cinema, print and billboards. Reaches a wide audience to build awareness, but is expensive and hard to measure precisely.

More targeted, controllable promotion the firm runs directly — sales promotions, direct mail, sponsorship, PR, personal selling and much digital marketing. Cheaper to target and easier to measure than ATL.
The combination of promotional methods (advertising, sales promotion, personal selling, public relations, direct and digital marketing) a business uses to communicate with its target market.

Unconventional, low-cost, high-impact promotion designed to surprise and generate word of mouth — flash mobs, striking stunts or street art. Favoured by smaller firms because impact per dollar can be high.

Everyone involved in delivering the service — frontline staff and management. Because production and consumption happen together, their skill, attitude and appearance ARE part of the product the customer experiences.
The systems and procedures by which a service is delivered — booking, queuing, payment, after-sales. A smooth, reliable process reduces the variability that makes services hard to trust.

The tangible cues that make an intangible service assessable — premises, décor, uniforms, packaging, websites, receipts. It 'tangibilises the intangible', giving customers proof of quality before and after purchase.

Penetration = LOW launch price to grab market share (mass market, close substitutes). Skimming = HIGH launch price to profit from early adopters (innovative, differentiated products). Reversing these is a classic error.
Place is the whole DISTRIBUTION CHANNEL — direct, wholesale, retail, e-commerce and logistics — not merely the address of a store. Reducing it to 'the shop' loses most of the marks.

quickCheck.prompt: A start-up launches a genuinely innovative smart-home gadget with no direct rival and high development costs to recover. Which launch pricing strategy fits best, and why?
options: Penetration pricing, because a low price grabs market share fast
Price skimming, because a high launch price exploits early adopters and recovers development costs
Cost-plus pricing, because it guarantees every unit is profitable
Competitive pricing, because it matches the market rate
quickCheck.answer: Price skimming, because a high launch price exploits early adopters and recovers development costs

quickCheck.prompt: A new soft-drink brand wants to win a large share of a crowded market full of close substitutes as quickly as possible. Which pricing strategy is most appropriate?
options: Price skimming, to signal premium quality
Premium pricing, to build an exclusive image
Penetration pricing, to attract price-sensitive buyers and gain share fast
Dynamic pricing, to change the price every hour
quickCheck.answer: Penetration pricing, to attract price-sensitive buyers and gain share fast

quickCheck.prompt: What are the four stages of the product life cycle, in the correct order?
options: Introduction, growth, maturity, decline
Research, launch, profit, saturation
Development, introduction, boom, recession
Growth, maturity, decline, extension

quickCheck.prompt: Which of the following is an example of ABOVE-the-line promotion?
options: A sponsored stall handing out free samples in a shopping centre
A national television advertising campaign
An email newsletter sent to existing customers
A buy-one-get-one-free sales promotion at the till

quickCheck.prompt: A management consultancy is reviewing its marketing mix. Which set correctly names the THREE extra service Ps it should add to the original four?
options: Packaging, positioning, partnerships
People, process, physical evidence
Promotion, price, place
Productivity, planning, perception

faq.q: What is the difference between penetration pricing and price skimming?
faq.a: They are opposite launch strategies. Penetration pricing sets a deliberately LOW price to win market share quickly, which suits mass markets with close substitutes and price-sensitive customers. Price skimming sets a deliberately HIGH price to profit from early adopters and recover development costs, then lowers it as rivals arrive — which suits innovative, differentiated products with little immediate competition. Choosing the wrong one for the context (for example skimming a me-too product in a crowded market) is a classic exam error.

faq.q: Does 'Place' just mean the location of the shop?
faq.a: No. Place means the whole distribution channel — the route the product takes from producer to consumer. That can be direct (producer straight to consumer, e.g. a factory website), or indirect through wholesalers and/or retailers, and today very often e-commerce, marketplaces and apps. The physical address of a store is only one small part of it; logistics, intermediaries and online channels are all 'Place' decisions.

faq.q: Why were the three extra Ps added, and do they apply only to services?
faq.a: The extended mix (people, process, physical evidence) was added because services are intangible, inseparable (produced and consumed at the same time), variable and perishable, so the four original Ps do not fully capture how customers judge them. People, process and physical evidence manage exactly those problems. They were designed for services, but they increasingly matter for physical goods too — the staff who advise in a tech store (people), the smoothness of online ordering and delivery (process) and store layout or packaging (physical evidence) all shape the experience of buying a physical product.

faq.q: Which single P is the most important?
faq.a: There is no single most important P, and saying so usually loses marks. Their relative importance depends on the industry, product and target market: price may dominate for a budget airline, people for a consultancy, physical evidence for a luxury hotel. The examiner rewards the argument that the Ps are interdependent and must be consistent — a great product priced, placed and promoted in ways that contradict it will still fail — not a claim that one dial matters more than all the others.

faq.q: Is the marketing mix for a physical good the same as for a service?
faq.a: The framework is the same, but the emphasis shifts. For a physical good the four original Ps carry most of the weight, and physical evidence often lives in the product and packaging themselves. For a service, because there is nothing tangible to inspect before purchase, the three extra Ps do heavy lifting: people deliver and often ARE the product, process reduces the variability customers fear, and physical evidence supplies the tangible proof of quality that a physical good would provide on its own. A strong answer explains WHY the balance changes, not just that it does.

sections.content: Once a business has researched its market and chosen a target segment, it has to decide the concrete actions that will win and keep customers. Those actions are the marketing mix. Originally the four Ps — product, price, place and promotion — the mix was extended with three more Ps — people, process and physical evidence — to capture how customers actually judge services, which cannot be seen or touched before purchase. This lesson covers the whole of syllabus 4.5: each element in turn, including the product life cycle, the Boston matrix and branding under Product, and the full set of pricing strategies under Price; how the seven elements must be integrated and consistent; and how the mix differs for goods versus services. Because 4.5 questions reward application and evaluation, the worked examples are set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks, so you can see why a mix applied to the specific business and a supported judgement outscore a memorised list every time.
Product: the life cycle, the portfolio and the brand

Product is the offering itself — the physical good or intangible service — including its features, quality, design, packaging and branding. But 'Product' in the syllabus is more than a description of the item; it is a set of decisions that change over the product's life and across the firm's whole range. Three ideas do most of the analytical work: the product life cycle, the product portfolio (Boston) matrix and branding.

items: **The product life cycle (PLC):** most products pass through four stages — **introduction** (launch, low sales, heavy promotion, often losses), **growth** (rising sales and profit, competitors appear), **maturity** (sales peak and level off, competition is fierce, price pressure) and **decline** (sales fall as the product is superseded). The marketing mix must change with the stage — for example promotion is informative at launch but persuasive/reminder-based at maturity.

**Extension strategies:** actions taken to prolong the maturity stage and delay decline — new features, new packaging, new markets, price changes or fresh advertising — so the firm keeps earning from an established product.

**The Boston (product portfolio) matrix:** plots each product by market share and market growth into four groups — **stars** (high share, high growth: invest), **cash cows** (high share, low growth: milk for funds), **question marks/problem children** (low share, high growth: decide whether to back) and **dogs** (low share, low growth: consider dropping). It guides how a multi-product firm spreads investment and balances risk.

**Branding:** a distinctive identity — name, logo, values and promise — that customers recognise and trust. Strong brands differentiate the product, justify premium pricing, build loyalty and add value beyond the physical features (brand awareness, brand loyalty and brand value).

sections.content: These tools connect: a 'star' in the Boston matrix is often a product in the growth stage of its life cycle, and a strong brand is what lets a firm extend a mature product's life or charge a premium price. The examiner rewards students who link Product decisions to the rest of the mix rather than treating each idea in isolation.
Price: choosing a strategy, not just a number

Price is the amount the customer pays, and it is the only element of the mix that directly earns revenue — the others cost money. A price must cover costs over time, sit sensibly against competitors, and match what the target customer is willing to pay, all while reinforcing the brand's positioning. The syllabus expects you to know a range of pricing strategies and, crucially, to judge which suits a given situation.

items: **Cost-plus pricing:** add a fixed mark-up to unit cost. Simple and guarantees each unit covers cost, but ignores competitors and customers' willingness to pay.

**Penetration pricing:** a LOW launch price to win market share quickly. Suits mass markets, price-sensitive buyers and products with close substitutes; sacrifices margin for volume and market presence.

**Price skimming:** a HIGH launch price aimed at early adopters, lowered over time as rivals appear. Suits innovative, differentiated products with little initial competition and recovers high development costs fast.
**Competitive pricing:** price at or around the going market rate. Common where products are similar and no single firm can charge much more than rivals.
**Psychological pricing:** prices set to feel lower or more appealing — $9.99 rather than $10 — using perception rather than a real cost saving.
**Premium pricing:** a consistently HIGH price to signal superior quality, exclusivity or status. Works only if the brand and the other Ps genuinely support that image.
**Dynamic pricing:** continuously adjusting price to real-time demand, timing or customer data (airline seats, surge fares, event tickets). Maximises revenue but risks appearing unfair.

sections.content: Never recommend a pricing strategy in the abstract. The marks come from matching the strategy to the specific product and market: is it innovative or a me-too product, is the market crowded or new, are customers price-sensitive or status-driven, and does the price agree with the brand's positioning? A luxury product on a penetration price contradicts its own Product and Promotion — spotting that inconsistency is exactly the AO3 reasoning examiners reward.
Place: getting the product to the customer

Place is the distribution channel — the route a product travels from producer to consumer — not simply the location of a shop. A business chooses how directly to sell and which intermediaries, if any, to use, balancing reach, cost and control.

items: **Direct channel:** producer sells straight to the consumer (factory outlet, own website, direct sales force). Keeps the full margin and the customer relationship, but the producer must handle logistics and reach.

**Indirect channels:** the product passes through intermediaries — a **retailer** (producer → retailer → consumer), or a **wholesaler and retailer** (producer → wholesaler → retailer → consumer). Intermediaries extend reach and share the selling burden, but each takes a margin and dilutes control.
**E-commerce and digital channels:** websites, online marketplaces and apps allow producers to reach large audiences directly and cheaply, reshaping distribution across most industries.

**Channel choice must fit the mix:** an exclusive premium product sold through discount channels undermines its own positioning, while a mass-market product needs wide, convenient distribution to hit its volume targets.

sections.content: Promotion: the promotional mix, above/below the line and beyond

Promotion is how the business communicates with its target market to inform, persuade and remind. The set of methods it uses is the promotional mix, and those methods are traditionally split into above-the-line and below-the-line — with guerrilla and digital marketing increasingly important, especially for smaller firms.

items: **Above-the-line (ATL):** paid promotion through mass, largely untargeted media — TV, radio, cinema, print, billboards. Builds wide awareness quickly but is expensive and hard to measure precisely.

**Below-the-line (BTL):** more targeted, controllable methods run by the firm — sales promotions (discounts, BOGOF, loyalty schemes), direct mail, sponsorship, public relations (PR), personal selling and much digital activity. Cheaper to target and easier to measure.
**The promotional mix:** the blend of advertising, sales promotion, personal selling, public relations and direct/digital marketing chosen to suit the product, budget and audience.

**Guerrilla marketing:** unconventional, low-cost, high-impact tactics — stunts, flash mobs, street art — designed to surprise and spark word of mouth; popular with smaller firms because impact per dollar can be high.

**Digital marketing:** social media, search and display advertising, influencer partnerships, content and email. It is highly targetable and measurable, and blurs the ATL/BTL split — a viral social campaign can reach mass audiences at BTL cost.

sections.content: The three service Ps: people, process and physical evidence

Services are intangible (nothing to inspect before purchase), inseparable (produced and consumed at the same moment), variable (quality depends on who delivers them and when) and perishable (an unsold seat or appointment is lost forever). The four original Ps do not fully manage these characteristics, so three more were added to the mix specifically to market services well.

items: **People:** everyone involved in delivering the service — frontline staff and management. Because production and consumption happen together, their skills, attitude and appearance ARE part of the product the customer experiences. This makes recruitment, training and motivation marketing decisions, not just HR ones.

**Process:** the systems and procedures through which the service is delivered — booking, queuing, ordering, payment, after-sales support. A smooth, reliable, quick process reduces the variability customers fear and raises satisfaction.

**Physical evidence:** the tangible cues that let customers judge an intangible service — premises, décor, staff uniforms, packaging, website design, brochures and receipts. It 'tangibilises the intangible', giving proof of quality before and after purchase.

sections.content: The mix for goods versus services, and why integration matters

The seven Ps are not a checklist to tick off in isolation; they are interdependent and must be consistent. Every element should send the same message about what the brand is and who it is for. A luxury product (Product) sold cheaply (Price) in a discount store (Place) with cut-price flyers (Promotion) contradicts itself, and customers are left confused. The mix also shifts in emphasis between goods and services: for a physical good the four original Ps carry most of the weight and much of the physical evidence lives in the product and its packaging; for a service the three extra Ps do the heavy lifting, supplying the reliability, human contact and tangible proof that an intangible offering otherwise lacks. Strong exam answers explain WHY the balance changes, not merely that it does.
Effective marketing = Consistency across (Product + Price + Place + Promotion + People + Process + Physical Evidence)

sections.question: A new start-up, 'Streamly', is launching a video-streaming service targeting university students with a curated library of independent films for a low monthly fee. Explain how three elements of the marketing mix could be designed to suit Streamly's target market. [6]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
**Price.** Streamly should use penetration pricing — a low monthly subscription (for example around $5) — possibly with a free trial. Its target market is price-sensitive students, and the streaming market contains strong substitutes such as mainstream platforms, so a low price attracts sign-ups quickly and builds the subscriber base the business needs to survive.

**Promotion.** Digital and below-the-line promotion fits both the audience and a start-up budget: targeted social-media advertising on the platforms students use, partnerships with university film societies and student brand ambassadors. This reaches the exact segment cheaply and is easy to measure, unlike costly above-the-line TV advertising.

**Process.** Because streaming is a service, the process must be frictionless — a simple sign-up and payment, an intuitive interface and reliable, high-quality playback. A smooth process reduces the variability customers fear in a service and keeps price-sensitive students from cancelling.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 6 marks** — the command term 'explain' calls for AO2 application, so each of the three elements must be developed and tied to Streamly, not left generic:

- **AO1 (×3, 1 each):** identifies a valid mix element correctly — penetration pricing, digital/BTL promotion, a service 'process'.
- **AO2 (×3, 1 each):** develops each in Streamly's context — a low price for price-sensitive students in a substitute-rich market; targeted social/ambassador promotion on a start-up budget; a frictionless sign-up and reliable playback because it is a service.

The engine credits developed, applied points: naming 'penetration pricing' with no link to student price-sensitivity caps that point at the AO1 mark and forfeits the AO2 application mark.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Reversing penetration and skimming** — penetration is a LOW launch price to grab share in a mass, substitute-rich market; skimming is a HIGH launch price to profit from early adopters of an innovative, differentiated product. Swapping them wrecks the answer.
**Reducing 'Place' to the shop's location** — Place is the whole distribution channel (direct, wholesale, retail, e-commerce and logistics), not merely a street address.

**Getting the product life cycle stages wrong or out of order** — the four stages are introduction, growth, maturity and decline; confusing them, or omitting extension strategies at maturity, loses easy AO1 marks.

**Muddling above- and below-the-line promotion** — above-the-line is paid mass media (TV, radio, print, billboards); below-the-line is targeted, firm-controlled activity (sales promotions, sponsorship, PR, direct and much digital marketing).
**Forgetting or mislabelling the three service Ps** — the extra Ps are people, process and physical evidence, added for services; naming 'packaging' or 'positioning' instead is a common slip.
**Claiming one P is simply 'the most important'** — the marks reward integration and consistency across the mix and application to the specific business, not a bare ranking.
**Listing the Ps without applying them** — a generic list of the 7 Ps earns AO1 only; the marks climb when each element is tied to the business in the case.
**Recommending a mix or pricing strategy with no supported judgement** — a 'discuss' or 'recommend' answer that gives both sides but never commits to a justified conclusion cannot reach the top band.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Business Management 4.5 is assessed against three objectives: AO1 rewards relevant knowledge of the mix and pricing concepts, AO2 rewards applying that knowledge to the specific business in the stimulus, and AO3 rewards analysis and a balanced evaluation. In the analytic/points scheme each distinct valid point earns credit, but the higher 'discuss', 'evaluate' and 'recommend' marks are reserved for answers that combine APPLICATION to context with a BALANCED evaluation ending in a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT. Watch how the marks below attach to applied, two-sided reasoning and a justified conclusion — never to a generic list.

sections.question: Discuss the most appropriate pricing strategy for a business launching an innovative new technology product. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
For a genuinely innovative technology product — say a wearable health device with no direct competitor — the two realistic launch strategies are price skimming and penetration pricing, and the choice turns on the product's novelty, its cost structure and the type of customer it first attracts.

Skimming argues for a high launch price. An innovative product with no close substitute gives the firm temporary market power, and there is usually a segment of early adopters — technology enthusiasts — willing to pay a premium to own it first. A high price lets the business recover the large research-and-development costs typical of technology products quickly, while the high price itself signals cutting-edge quality and reinforces the brand's innovative positioning. As rivals inevitably copy the technology, the firm can lower the price in steps to reach more price-sensitive segments, having already 'skimmed' the most profitable customers. This directly supports a differentiated, premium product with heavy development costs to recoup.

Penetration pricing argues the opposite: a low launch price to win a large market share fast. In technology markets, network effects and ecosystems matter — the more users adopt a platform, the more valuable it becomes and the harder it is for later rivals to displace. A low price would build that installed base quickly and could deter competitors from entering at all. However, for a product described as innovative and differentiated, penetration sacrifices the premium margin the firm needs to recover development costs, and a low price can even undermine the perception of quality that makes an innovative product desirable.

The context is decisive. Penetration suits mass markets full of close substitutes and price-sensitive buyers — the reverse of a novel product with no direct rival. Skimming suits exactly the situation described: an innovative, differentiated product with high development costs and identifiable early adopters. There is a caveat: if the technology is easy to imitate, a high price invites fast competition, so the firm should skim while planning staged price cuts, and pricing must stay consistent with the rest of the mix — a premium price alongside strong branding, selective distribution and targeted promotion to enthusiasts.

**Judgement.** On balance, price skimming is the most appropriate launch strategy for an innovative new technology product. The decisive factors are the product's novelty and its high development costs: with little or no direct competition, a high launch price captures the early adopters' willingness to pay, recovers R&D quickly and reinforces the innovative brand image, while the main risk — attracting imitators — is managed by lowering the price over time as competition arrives. Penetration would be the better choice only if the technology were easily copied and network effects made rapid mass adoption more valuable than early margin; absent that, skimming outweighs it for this context.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 10 marks:**

- **AO1 — knowledge (pricing-strategy concepts):** the engine credits accurate command of the strategies the question turns on — skimming and penetration in particular, plus the reasoning about early adopters, R&D recovery, market power and network effects. Correct terminology signals AO1.
- **AO2 — application to THIS product:** marks are awarded for tying those concepts to an innovative technology product specifically — no direct substitute, high development costs to recover, early-adopter enthusiasts willing to pay a premium, imitation risk over time. Generic definitions that never mention the innovative product do not earn AO2.
- **AO3 — analysis and balanced evaluation:** the engine credits developed two-sided reasoning — skimming's premium margin and quality signal weighed against penetration's share-grabbing and network-effect advantages — and, crucially, a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT that commits to skimming and justifies WHY it outweighs penetration for this context.
- **What lifts an answer into the top band:** application plus judgement. The engine rewards weighing skimming against penetration for THIS product and then deciding with a reason; it does NOT reward a longer generic list of every pricing strategy. A response that defines all seven pricing strategies but never applies them and never chooses is held in the lower bands, however much it contains.
- **What the engine rejects:** one-sided answers that only argue for skimming or only for penetration; a 'judgement' that restates both sides without choosing; and correct definitions with no link to the innovative technology product.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — AO1 for the right pricing and mix concepts, AO2 for applying them to the business in the question, and AO3 for balanced analysis ending in a supported judgement. So on a 'discuss' pricing question, do not just define every strategy: name the two or three that genuinely fit, apply them to the specific product and market, weigh them against each other, and finish by committing to the most appropriate one with a reason. The application and the judgement are where the top-band marks are won.

sections.question: Analyse the importance of people, process and physical evidence for a luxury hotel chain. [6]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
For a luxury hotel the three extra service Ps are central to its competitive advantage, because a hotel stay is intangible and the guest judges it almost entirely on the experience.

**People.** Highly trained, attentive staff — concierges, receptionists, housekeeping — deliver the personal service that defines luxury and justifies the high price. Because the service is produced and consumed at the same moment, the staff effectively ARE the product, so their professionalism directly creates (or destroys) the guest's perception of value.

**Process.** A seamless journey from online booking to swift check-in, prompt room service and effortless check-out reduces the variability guests fear in any service. Friction — a long queue, a slow response — instantly contradicts the luxury promise, so a smooth process protects the premium positioning.

**Physical evidence.** The grand lobby, quality furnishings, immaculate uniforms and crisp linen supply the tangible proof of luxury that an intangible service otherwise lacks, reinforcing the premium brand and reassuring guests before and during their stay.

**Analysis.** The three Ps are interdependent: well-trained people execute a seamless process within luxurious physical evidence, producing one consistent, premium experience that differentiates the hotel from cheaper rivals and builds loyalty. It is the integration, not any single P, that sustains the competitive advantage and supports the high price.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 6 marks:**

- **AO1 (up to 2):** correctly identifies and defines people, process and physical evidence as the service Ps.
- **AO2 (up to 2):** applies each to the luxury hotel — trained concierges, seamless check-in/out, the grand lobby and furnishings.
- **AO3 (up to 2):** analyses HOW they interact and why their consistency (not one P alone) creates the competitive advantage and justifies the premium price. A list of the three Ps with no application or linkage stays in the lower bands.

sections.content: Where this leads

The marketing mix ties the whole marketing unit together: the target market chosen in market segmentation decides which mix will resonate; the product life cycle and Boston matrix feed directly into decisions about extension strategies and new-product development; and pricing and promotion choices reappear in sales forecasting and in the ethical and international marketing topics. Master the habit built here — identify the mix concept, apply it to the specific business, weigh the options, then commit to a justified judgement — and you have the template that earns marks across every application and evaluation question in Business Management.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 question marked: recommend or discuss a marketing mix (or pricing strategy) for a business, applying the 7 Ps and reaching a supported judgement
